interpretations to be used in the development and implementation of engineering drawings for
the TCF school construction.

All Engineering personnel and their contractors shall follow the requirements and guidelines

Comments or inquiries concerning this manual should be directed to the Engineering
Department.

Objective

The Citizens Foundation (TCF) professionally builds and manages hundreds of purpose-built
school units across Pakistan. The intent of the Engineering Standards Manual is to present clear
and concise directions regarding technical requirements and processes to facilitate construction
of TCF schools. Therefore, the Engineering Standards Manual provides uniform standards of
construction to ensure accuracy and provides guidelines which are to be followed by project
managers, ACM’s and contractors.

With this engineering manual, it is expected that all TCF construction will follow a dedicated
pattern in relevance with circumstances, context and construction details.

DESIGN PHILOSOPHY

Design Philosophy

TCF believes that school building should be able to fulfill all functional and practical needs for
its students and staff. A proper school building provides all relevant facilities for academic and
extra-curricular activities. TCF schools use space in a manner that they remain open, flexible to
change and possible to explore. At the same time, TCF takes great care in designing school units
according to the geographical and climatic needs of the area.

Our design philosophy is based on the following functional and aesthetical criteria:

e Quality

Low Maintenance

Climate Consideration
Daylight and Ventilation

Designs incorporate ample space for play areas and courtyards that add value to school
structures. There is a balance between order and spontaneity that renders spaces open to change.
There is a conscious effort to maximize the use of daylight in order to create bright classrooms
and corridors to save energy costs. Climate is also taken into consideration and sustainable
approach to materials selection for quality enhancement is a must for TCF construction rules.

Design Parameters:

Every effort is made to keep construction costs minimum as well as keeping the design simple,
comfortable and open. Some economical local materials such as pigmented cement pl

the design simple,
comfortable and open. Some economical local materials such as pigmented cement plaster,
concrete Jali, checkered tiles as welcome mats, glazed clay tiles strips, etc are used to add detail
and color to the interiors.

Particular care is taken to provide durable and maintenance free finishes. Schools in villages are
constructed of brick masonry with pointing on exteriors, and schools in dense, urban areas are
generally finished with pigmented cement-sand plaster in terracotta colors that users are
comfortable with and which render the building easily identifiable.

LAYOUT CONSIDERATIONS

LAYOUT CONSIDERATIONS

TCF school buildings are conceived to accommodate and support a range of human activities in
response to social, cultural and economical needs. Therefore it is imperative to evaluate the need
of the location and the environment before building on site.

Parameters:

Parameters that define layout and planning of TCF schools mainly include:

Effective utilization of plot/area.

Usually, a plot of lacre is considered

schools mainly include:

Effective utilization of plot/area.

Usually, a plot of lacre is considered as sufficient area to fulfill the needs of 2P and 28,
whereas plot dimensions and outline varies respectively.

Ample space provision for play area is important role in defining layout of our schools.
Climatic conditions, orientation of the building with regard to the path of the sun and
wind direction are considered in layout of our schools.

Circulation and access route define position of the layout.

Building is built considering the shape of the plot.

In rare cases, social and cultural issues of local community force the change in layout and
planning.

Site Survey:

Proper site investigation at the initial stage should be carried out using the standard site survey
form to ensure proper investigation before design and layout. (Refer attached Site Survey form)

Soil Testing:

ACM should immediately inform the Engineering Department if there is a need for soil testing
after site survey. The following steps should be taken:

@

Sites should be checked visually first to find the feasibility for soil investigation.

Type of the soil can be easily checked through simple methods. One of the methods,
called the rope test, requires that you squeeze a moist, but not muddy, one inch ball of
soil in your hand. Then rub the soil between your fingers.

a moist, but not muddy, one inch ball of
soil in your hand. Then rub the soil between your fingers. Sandy soil feels gritty and
loose. It won't form a ball and falls apart when rubbed between your fingers. Loam soil is
smooth, slick, partially gritty and sticky and forms a ball that crumbles easily. It is a
combination of sand and clay particles. Clay soil is smooth, sticky and somewhat plastic
feeling. It forms ribbons when pressed between fingers. Clay soil requires more pressure
to form a ball than loam soil, but does not crumble apart as easily.

Clay soil and loam soil lead to difficult soil conditions for construction and usually
require proper soil testing.

Also check type of foundation and footing constructed in the vicinity areas to form a
tentative opinion.

For new areas, soil

tion and footing constructed in the vicinity areas to form a
tentative opinion.

For new areas, soil testing needs to be carried out if conditions seem inappropriate to
evaluate soil bearing capacity. Data can be used for all the similar sites nearby.

Obtain information regarding physical properties such as porosity, permeability, moisture
content, soil texture and soil structure etc.

Masonry and Floor Slab:

Block masonry and floor slab construction should be as per drawing and should be coordinated
with structural drawing as well.

e Proper room size should be maintained b

e Corners should be properly checked by accurate angles.

e Ensure proper beam to beam joints.

e The concrete in columns beams and slab should be of the ratio 1:2:4 (1 part cement, 2
parts sand and 4 parts gravel).

e The optimum quantity of water may be used for making good quality concrete. The
quantity of water should not be more than 5 to 6 tin (5 kg empty oil tin) for one bag of
cement (50Kg) in the concrete prepared for columns, beams and slab. If gravel and sand
is already wet, then reduce the amount of water. Remember the use of excess water is
extremely harmful for concrete strength.

e A simple way to assess the quantity of water in the concrete i

ely harmful for concrete strength.

e A simple way to assess the quantity of water in the concrete is that if it is taken in hand
and pressed, good quality concrete cement slurry does not flow out of the fingers.

e Maximum thickness of mortar between two courses should not be more than 3/8”.
Thicker the mortar layer, weaker the masonry work would be.

e Minimum 7 days curing is required for masonry work. Use drinking water for curing.
Improper curing may reduce the strength of wall significantly.
